range.nativeRange: Central to southeastern United States — native across much of the eastern prairie and woodland edge communities, from Kansas and Oklahoma east through the Appalachians and into the mid-Atlantic

usNativeStatus.description: Native — broadly native across the eastern and central US; listed as a species of conservation interest in some northern and western states where it is present but uncommon

invasive.description: Not invasive anywhere in the lower 48 US states; spreads mildly by self-seeding but is well-behaved and ecologically appropriate in cultivated settings

description.timeToMaturity: Blooms well from second season; medicinally productive from year 3+
description.lifespan: Long-lived; 10-20+ years; self-sows to maintain colony
description.deciduousEvergreen: Herbaceous — dies back in winter; seedheads persist
description.winterInterest: Moderate — the spiky bronze seedheads are ornamentally attractive and provide winter bird food; leave standing through winter

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun preferred; tolerates part shade (blooms somewhat less heavily, may get slightly leggy)
lightAndWater.water: Medium; drought tolerant once established — one of the more drought-hardy native perennials

drainagePreference.description: Well-drained preferred; tolerates average; does not like prolonged wet feet

claySoilPerformance.description: Tolerates clay soils well once established — evolved on heavy prairie soils; drainage must not be so poor that roots sit in standing water for extended periods; raised planting or amendment on the heaviest sites is beneficial at establishment

soil.texturePreference: Adaptable across the lower 48; tolerates clay, loam, and lean sandy soils — evolved on prairie edges and woodland margins with variable soils; does not require rich soil and can perform poorly (excessive lush growth, floppy stems) if over-amended
soil.rootCharacter: Deep fibrous taproot; does not spread aggressively; slowly expands clump; self-seeds moderately

bloom.bloomDescription: Pink-purple ray petals (reflexed, drooping slightly) surrounding a prominent orange-bronze central cone; 3-4" diameter; flowers face outward and upward
bloom.pollination: Self-fertile; major pollinator plant — attracts bees, butterflies, and specialist native insects

yield.edibleParts: Petals (edible fresh or dried for tea); seeds (edible, mild)
yield.harvestSeason: Flowers July-September; roots harvestable from year 3+ in fall
yield.medicinalUses: One of the most extensively studied herbal immune-support plants; all parts used; root has highest concentration of active compounds (alkylamides, polysaccharides, cichoric acid); used for cold/flu prevention and reduction; also topically for wound healing; long documented use by Indigenous peoples of the Plains
yield.craftOtherYield: Dried flowers and seedheads in arrangements; natural dye (roots yield yellow-gold)

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityPeople: Non-toxic; edible and medicinal; not recommended for those with autoimmune conditions or ragweed allergies (same family)
cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: Generally considered safe; large quantities may cause mild GI upset

permaculture.coppiceable: Deadhead spent flowers to extend bloom; leave final flush of flowers to set seed for winter wildlife and self-renewal

wildlife.attracts: Bees (especially native bumblebees and sweat bees), butterflies (monarchs, swallowtails), goldfinches and other small birds (seeds through winter — one of the best native seed sources available)
wildlife.hostPlantFor: Host for several specialist native moths and butterflies; member of Asteraceae which supports hundreds of specialist native insects
wildlife.nestingHabitat: Pithy hollow stems provide overwintering habitat for native solitary bees if left standing through winter

deer.description: Moderate — browsed but not heavily targeted across most of its range; established plants recover well from light browse; in high-pressure deer environments, protection at establishment is advisable

drought.description: Good once established — deep taproot accesses subsoil moisture

tolerances.other: Tolerates poor soil, clay, heat, and neglect; very forgiving once established; performs across a wide range of US climate zones (3-9)

maintenance.pruningNotes: Deadhead for extended bloom or leave seedheads for wildlife (the better choice in most cases). Cut back to ground in early spring before new growth emerges. Divide every 4-5 years in spring to maintain vigor if clump becomes crowded, though division is not strictly necessary.
propagationMethods: Seed (self-sows readily; direct sow in fall for spring germination, or cold-stratify 30 days for spring sowing)

cultivarNotes:
The straight species performs well across zones 3-9 throughout the lower 48. Cultivar selection depends on bed constraints and regional conditions:
- Compact cultivars (18-24") suit height-limited beds or the front of any border: 'Kim's Knee High' (18-20"), 'PowWow Wild Berry' (18-24", vivid magenta-pink)
- Mid-size cultivars (24-30") are the most versatile all-around choice: 'Magnus' (Perennial Plant of the Year 1998 — excellent performance from zone 3 south through zone 8; wide flat petals, does not reflex as strongly as straight species)
- In hot, humid climates (zones 7-9, Southeast US), look for cultivars with confirmed powdery mildew resistance; Echinacea can struggle with mildew under humid conditions with poor air circulation
- For medicinal use, straight species or 'Magnus' are preferred over heavily bred ornamental cultivars, which may have lower concentrations of active compounds (alkylamides, polysaccharides)

gardenUse.designPlacement: Versatile across the lower 48 — suited to mid-zone border placement in full sun to part shade; strong vertical interest from spiky seedheads in the off-season; pairs well with ornamental grasses, Rudbeckia, Monarda, and Salvia; appropriate for formal borders, naturalistic meadows, and cottage-style plantings alike

missouriBotanicalGarden.url:
https://www.missouribotanicalgarden.org/PlantFinder/PlantFinderDetails.aspx?kempercode=c580
Pennsylvania — Hegins Site Observations:
- None yet

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Yes — native to Pennsylvania plant communities; found in woodland edges, meadows, and disturbed prairies throughout the state
pennsylvaniaHegins.paInvasiveStatus:
Not listed as invasive in Pennsylvania
Zone: 3-9
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Red clay soils at Hegins are workable for Echinacea with amended planting holes or raised berms; established plants handle compaction better than most; avoid low spots that collect water after rain
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Red clay loam at Hegins is well within the tolerance range; no aggressive amendment needed; light compost incorporation at planting is sufficient
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Straight species and 'Magnus' are the most reliable performers; 'Kim's Knee High' is a strong choice for Section A's 5' height limit; all three are cold-hardy well beyond zone 6a with no winter protection needed
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Section A (north-facing front bed, mid-zone — native medicinal anchor with strong winter seedhead interest; tolerates the partial shade and cold microclimate; fits the purple/pink palette); Section C (south-facing back porch, warm/dry — excellent performance in the warmer drier exposure, pairs well with Salvia yangii and Monarda didyma)
